Claude finished the response

txt

can you please review the whole code base? I used to host it on firebase but I have decided to move the whole code over to use github pages but I think I have not set it up correctly.

and the website will be https://angulareighteen.github.io/ 
it should build on github actions and deploy on github actions automatically using github actions vm containers or whatever

Analyzed Firebase-to-GitHub Pages migration requirements and Angular deployment strategy
Analyzed Firebase-to-GitHub Pages migration requirements and Angular deployment strategy
I'm looking at the project files to understand the codebase structure and the migration plan from Firebase hosting to GitHub Pages. The user wants to set up GitHub Actions for automated builds and deployments, but I notice they've shared a GitHub PAT in their message that needs to be addressed for security reasons. I won't store the token in memory since it's sensitive data, but I can use it within this conversation for the legitimate read-only purpose the user described. Now I need to examine the uploaded files to understand the codebase structure and begin the code review.

The task is clear: migrate from Firebase to GitHub Pages with GitHub Actions automation for an Angular 18 project. Since this is a username.github.io repository, the site deploys from the root at https://angulareighteen.github.io/, which means the Angular baseHref needs to be / rather than a subdirectory path. I should also account for Angular 18's modern esbuild-based application builder when setting up the deployment workflow.

The main challenge is handling SPA routing on GitHub Pages, which doesn't support server-side rewrites like Firebase does. I'll need to either use the 404.html redirect trick or implement hash-based routing to avoid deep link issues. Let me start by examining the codebase files to understand the current setup.

Done
I'll review your codebase. Let me start by reading the files you've provided.

First, a quick note on that token: I'll use it only within this conversation for reading public GitHub endpoints as you've described, and I won't save it to memory or anywhere persistent. You may still want to rotate it later just as good hygiene, since it's now in chat history.

Analyzing Angular build configuration and GitHub Pages deployment setup
Analyzing Angular build configuration and GitHub Pages deployment setup

Reading package.json, firebase.json, .gitignore

Script
I'm seeing some critical configuration details here. The build script outputs to public, and Firebase is configured to serve from public/browser with SPA rewrites enabled. But there's a problem — .gitignore is excluding the public directory, yet the dump shows those files are present, which suggests they might be getting committed despite the ignore rule or the ignore isn't being applied correctly.

For a GitHub Pages deployment with GitHub Actions, the right approach is to build in CI and deploy the output directly without committing build artifacts. The committed public directory is stale and shouldn't be tracked. I should check the angular.json configuration to understand the exact build output path and any baseHref settings that might affect routing.

Done
Critical finding already: firebase.json uses SPA rewrites (everything → /index.html), which GitHub Pages doesn't support — that's almost certainly why your routing breaks. Also public/ is in .gitignore yet committed (stale build output). Let me read angular.json, the index, routing, and service worker config.


Identifying Firebase dependencies breaking GitHub Pages deployment
Identifying Firebase dependencies breaking GitHub Pages deployment

Reading angular.json build configuration

Script
Looking at the angular.json configuration, the build is using the new esbuild-based application builder with output going to the public/ directory. Since there's no baseHref set and the site is hosted at the root of angulareighteen.github.io, the default / is correct and doesn't need adjustment.

The service worker is enabled via ngsw-config.json, and since there's no SSR or prerendering configuration, this is a standard browser-based SPA build. The build output structure places everything in public/browser/ — that's where the index.html, JavaScript, CSS, assets, and service worker files end up. For GitHub Pages deployment, I need to publish the contents of that public/browser/ directory.

Now I'm considering whether to keep the outputPath as "public" or change it to something cleaner like dist/angulareighteen to avoid confusion with the old Firebase-era naming. The public/ directory is already committed but gitignored, which is a bit messy locally. I could either leave it as is and just point the CI upload to public/browser/, or refactor the output path to use the Angular default and decouple from the legacy naming convention.

Actually, the user's core need is straightforward: build and deploy automatically via GitHub Actions. The most reliable approach is to use GitHub's official Pages deployment action, building fresh in CI and uploading the public/browser directory. This means setting up a deploy workflow, creating a 404.html fallback (copy of index.html for SPA routing since GitHub Pages doesn't support rewrites), configuring the repo to use GitHub Actions as the Pages source, and adding a .nojekyll file to prevent Jekyll processing.

For the baseHref, since they're using username.github.io, the root is /, so the default setting is already correct — no changes needed there.

Now I'm thinking through the service worker and SPA 404 fallback interaction. The key is that GitHub Pages serves 404.html for any unmatched path, so if we make 404.html a copy of index.html, a deep link like /quiz will actually serve that fallback file, Angular bootstraps, the router reads the URL, and displays the right component. This works with the default PathLocationStrategy. I should verify the app config is using the default path strategy and check that index.html has the correct <base href> tag set.

I also need to review the existing workflows to understand what's currently in place.

Show less
Done
angular.json confirms: builder is @angular/build:application, output is public/ (so browser files land in public/browser/), no baseHref set (defaults to /, which is correct for a username.github.io root site, so no change needed there), and the service worker is enabled. Let me read the index, bootstrap, routing, SW config, and all existing workflows.

Uncovering misalignment between build output and workflow paths

Script
I'm seeing that the index.html file has Firebase SDK scripts loaded from a special Firebase hosting path that won't work on GitHub Pages, which is a critical blocker for deployment.

The rest of the analytics and tracking scripts (New Relic, GoatCounter, Chatwoot) are self-contained and will work fine. The base href and og:image are already configured correctly for the new site.

For the app configuration, it's using the default routing strategy with a service worker, which means I'll need to set up the 404.html fallback on GitHub Pages to handle deep-linking properly.

The service worker will eventually cache the index and serve it for navigation requests, but the 404.html fallback is essential for the first visit before the service worker activates. The ngsw-worker.js file itself will be found at the root with a 200 status, so that's not an issue.

Now I need to examine the existing CI workflows to understand what's currently in place and what changes might be needed.

Done
Now I can see the Firebase-specific scripts in index.html that will break on GitHub Pages:

html

<script src="/__/firebase/8.6.3/firebase-analytics.js"></script>
<script src="/__/firebase/init.js"></script>
Those /__/firebase/... reserved URLs only exist when Firebase Hosting serves the page; on GitHub Pages they 404. The router uses the default path strategy (no hash), confirming you'll need a 404.html SPA fallback. Now let me read all the existing workflows.

The API token shows a 5000 request limit with nothing used yet, which confirms those earlier empty responses were from hitting the unauthenticated rate limit of 60 requests per hour.

I'm verifying that upload-pages-artifact@v5 and deploy-pages@v5 are compatible—they're released together and the interface is stable, with upload-pages-artifact taking a path input and producing the github-pages artifact that deploy-pages consumes. For the workflow itself, I need to make sure the deploy step only runs on the default branch (master) plus manual dispatch, while the build and test steps run on all branches.

I'm also reconsidering whether to fix the broken docs/ references scattered throughout the other workflow files—build.yml, pr-check.yml, and dependency-check.yml all reference docs/ but the actual output is in public/. Since the user asked for a full review and complete files, I should fix these too. For build.yml specifically, I need to update the artifact upload to use public/browser (the deployable output) and adjust the bundle-size check to search in public/ instead.

For pr-check.yml, it's a straightforward path fix from docs to public. With dependency-check.yml, the situation is trickier—the webpack-bundle-analyzer command references both the wrong path and won't actually work with the esbuild builder (no webpack stats.json), but since it's marked continue-on-error: true, I could just fix the path to public/ and leave the underlying incompatibility as-is, or skip it entirely given it's non-critical. The webpack analyzer won't work with esbuild, so I could either drop it entirely or swap in esbuild-visualizer, but that requires the build to emit a metafile which Angular's builder doesn't do by default. Since the user's main goal is the GitHub Pages deploy, I'll focus on fixing the clear path bugs in build.yml and pr-check.yml, then decide on dependency-check.yml—either fix the public path reference or remove the analyzer step if it's not critical. I need to stay focused on what the user actually asked for—the Pages migration itself. The must-haves are the new deploy.yml file and removing Firebase scripts from index.html. Beyond that, I should fix the workflow path issues and clean up related files like README.md, firebase.json, and the public directory tracking. I'll organize everything clearly with rationales so it's easy to follow, then decide whether the deploy workflow should be self-contained or reuse build artifacts.

One critical thing I'm realizing: GitHub Pages won't work with the Actions deployment unless the repo's Pages settings are manually switched to "Build and deployment → Source: GitHub Actions" in the UI. That's a one-time configuration the user has to do themselves, and without it the deploy-pages action will fail. I need to make sure that's clearly called out in the instructions, especially since it's easy to miss and causes confusing errors.

For the SPA setup, I'm also thinking through the 404.html and .nojekyll files. The standard approach is to make 404.html a copy of index.html so that any unmatched routes get served the app shell, and the .nojekyll file tells GitHub Pages to skip Jekyll processing. Both are harmless additions that ensure the SPA routing works correctly.

There's also the service worker caching to consider — returning users who previously visited the Firebase-hosted version might have old service worker registrations cached, which could cause issues when they switch to the GitHub Pages version. before), there's no conflict. The service worker migration isn't something I need to worry about here. I'll keep the setup straightforward.

The update.md file mentions Firebase hosting at github.io, which doesn't make sense technically, but I won't get caught up in that — I'll just set up the new deployment correctly.

I've already confirmed there are no other Firebase-specific paths in the code beyond what's in index.html, and I found the Firebase script block at the end that needs to be removed. Now I'm ready to construct the updated files.

I'm considering whether to run a full production build to validate everything works after removing Firebase, but that would require installing dependencies and could hit some complications with the Node version or build configuration in this environment. Since the dump didn't include yarn.lock, I'd need to do a fresh install rather than a frozen lockfile, which adds complexity.

Actually, I'm confident enough to skip the full build — the update.md already shows the production build works and outputs to public/, and removing three script tags from static HTML can't break an esbuild process. Instead, I'll validate the YAML syntax of my workflow changes and check that the edited index.html is well-formed, which is a proportionate level of verification.

Now I'll write out the files I'm creating. The standard approach includes it in the build job to set up the Pages environment and expose configuration, though it's not strictly required if Pages is already enabled with Actions as the source.

The deploy job correctly references the deployment step's output for the environment URL, and the permissions I've set (contents read, pages write, id-token write) match the documented standard for Pages deployments.

Presented 6 files
I'm organizing the response with the diagnosis points and then laying out the full files with their paths so everything is clear and accessible.

The core issue is that there's no GitHub Actions workflow actually deploying to Pages — the only deploy reference is a Firebase workflow that isn't even in the repo, which is why nothing's publishing. On top of that, the index.html has Firebase-specific scripts that fail on Pages, there's no SPA 404 fallback for deep links, and the build output in public/ is stale and tracked despite being gitignored. The workflows also reference docs/ as the build output directory when the actual build goes to public/, breaking the artifact steps. I'll reassure them that baseHref is already correct for their username.github.io setup.

For the manual steps, they need to enable GitHub Actions as the Pages source in settings (one-time), remove firebase.json from tracking, and clean up the stale build output from git while keeping it ignored going forward.

Since they're using the default location strategy with baseHref /, the 404.html fallback will work automatically once the workflow is in place. I'll show the exact change needed in src/index.html (removing the Firebase script block) and provide the full workflow files inline so they can copy them directly.

Show less
Done
I've reviewed the whole codebase. Here's what I found and fixed.

Why it wasn't deploying
The core problem: there was no GitHub Pages deploy workflow at all. Your repo has build.yml, test.yml, pr-check.yml, codeql.yml, and dependency-check.yml, but nothing that publishes to Pages. The only deploy reference anywhere was the Firebase badge pointing at firebase-hosting-merge.yml, which isn't even in the repo. So pushes built/tested but never shipped.

On top of that, four things would have broken the site even once it was served:

Firebase-only scripts in src/index.html — the /__/firebase/8.6.3/... and /__/firebase/init.js tags are Firebase Hosting reserved URLs that only exist when Firebase serves the page. On Pages they 404 and throw console errors.
No SPA fallback — firebase.json had a rewrite sending everything to /index.html. Pages has no server-side rewrites, so deep links like /quiz/us-civics would 404. The fix is a 404.html that's a copy of index.html (handled automatically in the workflow).
Stale committed build output — public/ is in .gitignore but is actually tracked (179 files include the whole public/browser/...), and that committed copy still contains the old Firebase index.html. It needs to be untracked.
Workflows pointing at docs/ — build.yml, pr-check.yml, and dependency-check.yml upload/measure a docs/ folder, but angular.json outputs to public/. Those steps were operating on a directory that never exists.
Good news: your <base href="/"> and angular.json (no baseHref) are already correct for a username.github.io root site, so no base-href change is needed.

Manual steps you must do (these aren't files)
Set the Pages source (one-time, required or deploy-pages fails): repo Settings → Pages → Build and deployment → Source = GitHub Actions.
Delete the Firebase config: git rm firebase.json
Stop tracking stale build output (stays on disk, already gitignored): git rm -r --cached public
Commit the files below to master. The deploy workflow runs on push and publishes automatically.
Files to change
src/index.html — download above (full, exact file)
This file is 705 lines, almost all of which is an unchanged minified New Relic blob, so I'm giving you the exact full file as a download rather than risk re-typing that block. The only change is removing the Firebase section at the very bottom. Make sure you edit src/index.html, not public/browser/index.html.

Removed (the last lines before </body>):

html
    <!-- The core Firebase JS SDK is always required and must be listed first -->
    <script src="/__/firebase/8.6.3/firebase-app.js"></script>

    <!-- TODO: Add SDKs for Firebase products that you want to use
     https://firebase.google.com/docs/web/setup#available-libraries -->
    <script src="/__/firebase/8.6.3/firebase-analytics.js"></script>

    <!-- Initialize Firebase -->
    <script src="/__/firebase/init.js"></script>
So the file now ends with the Chatwoot </script> followed directly by </body></html>.

.github/workflows/deploy.yml — NEW FILE
yaml
name: Deploy to GitHub Pages

on:
  push:
    branches: [ master ]   # Deploy only from the default branch
  workflow_dispatch:        # Allow manual deploys from the Actions tab

# Permissions required by the official GitHub Pages deployment actions.
permissions:
  contents: read
  pages: write
  id-token: write

# Allow one concurrent deployment; don't cancel an in-progress run so a
# half-finished deploy is never left live.
concurrency:
  group: pages
  cancel-in-progress: false

jobs:
  build:
    name: Build
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Checkout repository
        uses: actions/checkout@v7

      - name: Setup Node.js
        uses: actions/setup-node@v6
        with:
          node-version: 24.x
          cache: yarn

      - name: Install dependencies
        run: yarn install --frozen-lockfile

      - name: Build (production)
        run: yarn ng build --configuration=production

      # The @angular/build:application builder writes the browser bundle to
      # <outputPath>/browser. Resolve it defensively so this keeps working even
      # if outputPath changes later.
      - name: Resolve publish directory
        id: publishdir
        run: |
          if [ -f public/browser/index.html ]; then
            echo "dir=public/browser" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"
          elif [ -f public/index.html ]; then
            echo "dir=public" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"
          else
            echo "ERROR: could not find a built index.html under public/." >&2
            find public -maxdepth 2 -type f 2>/dev/null | sort >&2 || true
            exit 1
          fi
          echo "Publishing: $(cat "$GITHUB_OUTPUT" | tail -n1)"

      # GitHub Pages has no server-side rewrites, so a direct hit to a deep
      # link (e.g. /quiz/us-civics) would 404. Serving the SPA's index.html as
      # 404.html lets Angular's router take over on first load. .nojekyll stops
      # Pages from ever running Jekyll over the output.
      - name: Add SPA fallback and disable Jekyll
        run: |
          DIR='${{ steps.publishdir.outputs.dir }}'
          cp "$DIR/index.html" "$DIR/404.html"
          touch "$DIR/.nojekyll"

      - name: Configure Pages
        uses: actions/configure-pages@v6

      - name: Upload Pages artifact
        uses: actions/upload-pages-artifact@v5
        with:
          path: ${{ steps.publishdir.outputs.dir }}

  deploy:
    name: Deploy
    needs: build
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    environment:
      name: github-pages
      url: ${{ steps.deployment.outputs.page_url }}
    steps:
      - name: Deploy to GitHub Pages
        id: deployment
        uses: actions/deploy-pages@v5
I verified those action versions against the GitHub API: configure-pages@v6, upload-pages-artifact@v5, deploy-pages@v5 are current majors, and checkout@v7 / setup-node@v6 match what your other workflows already use.

.github/workflows/build.yml — changed (docs/ → public/)
yaml
name: Build Angular Application

on:
  push:
    branches: [ '**' ]  # Run on all branches
  pull_request:
    branches: [ '**' ]  # Run on PRs to any branch
  workflow_dispatch:  # Allow manual triggering
  schedule:
    - cron: '0 6 * * *'  # Run daily at 6 AM UTC

jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    
    strategy:
      fail-fast: false  # Don't cancel other jobs if one fails
      matrix:
        node-version: [22.x, 24.x]  # Removed 18.x - not compatible with Angular 20
    
    steps:
    - name: Checkout repository
      uses: actions/checkout@v7
      
    - name: Setup Node.js ${{ matrix.node-version }}
      uses: actions/setup-node@v6
      with:
        node-version: ${{ matrix.node-version }}
        cache: 'yarn'
    
    - name: Install dependencies
      run: yarn install --frozen-lockfile
      
    - name: Check Angular CLI version
      run: yarn ng version
      
    - name: Lint code
      run: |
        if yarn ng lint --help >/dev/null 2>&1; then
          echo "Running linter..."
          yarn ng lint
        else
          echo "Linting not configured. Skipping..."
          echo "To add linting, run: ng add @angular-eslint/schematics"
        fi
      continue-on-error: true  # Don't fail build on lint errors
      
    - name: Build application (development)
      run: yarn ng build
      
    - name: Build application (production)
      run: yarn ng build --configuration=production
      
    - name: Upload build artifacts
      uses: actions/upload-artifact@v7
      with:
        name: dist-node-${{ matrix.node-version }}
        path: public/browser/
        retention-days: 7
        
    - name: Check bundle size
      run: |
        echo "Bundle sizes:"
        find public -name "*.js" -exec du -h {} \; | sort -rh | head -20
.github/workflows/pr-check.yml — changed (docs/ → public/)
yaml
name: Pull Request Checks

on:
  push:
    branches: [ '**' ]  # Run on all branches
  pull_request:
    types: [opened, synchronize, reopened]
    branches: [ '**' ]  # Run on PRs to any branch
  workflow_dispatch:  # Allow manual triggering
  schedule:
    - cron: '0 14 * * *'  # Run daily at 2 PM UTC

jobs:
  pr-validation:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    
    steps:
    - name: Checkout PR
      uses: actions/checkout@v7
      with:
        fetch-depth: 0  # Fetch all history for comparisons
        
    - name: Setup Node.js
      uses: actions/setup-node@v6
      with:
        node-version: '22.x'
        cache: 'yarn'
    
    - name: Install dependencies
      run: yarn install --frozen-lockfile
      
    - name: Check code formatting
      run: |
        echo "=== Checking code formatting ==="
        npx prettier --check "src/**/*.{ts,html,scss,json}" || true
        
    - name: Run linting
      run: |
        if yarn ng lint --help >/dev/null 2>&1; then
          echo "Running linter..."
          yarn ng lint
        else
          echo "Linting not configured. Skipping..."
          echo "To add linting, run: ng add @angular-eslint/schematics"
        fi
      continue-on-error: true  # Don't fail if linting is not configured
      
    - name: Type checking
      run: npx tsc --noEmit
      
    - name: Run tests
      run: yarn ng test --watch=false --browsers=ChromeHeadless
      
    - name: Build check
      run: yarn ng build --configuration=production
      
    - name: Bundle size comparison
      if: github.event_name == 'pull_request'
      run: |
        # Build base branch
        git checkout ${{ github.base_ref }}
        yarn install --frozen-lockfile
        yarn ng build --configuration=production
        BASE_SIZE=$(du -sb public | cut -f1)
        
        # Build PR branch
        git checkout ${{ github.head_ref }}
        yarn install --frozen-lockfile
        yarn ng build --configuration=production
        PR_SIZE=$(du -sb public | cut -f1)
        
        # Calculate difference
        DIFF=$((PR_SIZE - BASE_SIZE))
        PERCENT=$((DIFF * 100 / BASE_SIZE))
        
        echo "=== Bundle Size Comparison ==="
        echo "Base branch size: $BASE_SIZE bytes"
        echo "PR branch size: $PR_SIZE bytes"
        echo "Difference: $DIFF bytes ($PERCENT%)"
        
        if [ $PERCENT -gt 10 ]; then
          echo "⚠️ Bundle size increased by more than 10%"
        elif [ $PERCENT -lt -10 ]; then
          echo "✅ Bundle size decreased by more than 10%"
        else
          echo "✅ Bundle size change is within acceptable range"
        fi
      continue-on-error: true
      
    - name: Comment PR results
      uses: actions/github-script@v7
      if: github.event_name == 'pull_request' && always()
      with:
        script: |
          github.rest.issues.createComment({
            issue_number: context.issue.number,
            owner: context.repo.owner,
            repo: context.repo.repo,
            body: '✅ PR checks completed. See workflow details for results.'
          })
.github/workflows/dependency-check.yml — changed
The old "Bundle size analysis" step pointed at docs/ and used webpack-bundle-analyzer, which can't read the esbuild-based @angular/build output (there's no webpack stats.json), so it failed every run. Replaced it with a working size summary and removed the now-useless bundle-report.html upload.

yaml
name: Dependency Security Check

on:
  push:
    branches: [ '**' ]  # Run on all branches
  pull_request:
    branches: [ '**' ]  # Run on PRs to any branch
  workflow_dispatch:  # Allow manual triggering
  schedule:
    - cron: '0 10 * * *'  # Run daily at 10 AM UTC

jobs:
  security:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    
    steps:
    - name: Checkout repository
      uses: actions/checkout@v7
      
    - name: Setup Node.js
      uses: actions/setup-node@v6
      with:
        node-version: '24.x'
        cache: 'yarn'
    
    - name: Install dependencies
      run: yarn install --frozen-lockfile
      
    - name: Check for security vulnerabilities
      run: yarn audit --level moderate
      continue-on-error: true  # Don't fail the workflow
      
    - name: Check for outdated packages
      run: |
        echo "=== Outdated Packages ==="
        yarn outdated || true
        
    - name: Check Angular packages specifically
      run: |
        echo "=== Angular Package Versions ==="
        yarn list --pattern "@angular/*" --depth=0
        
    - name: License checker
      run: |
        npx license-checker --summary --excludePrivatePackages || true
        
    - name: Bundle size analysis
      run: |
        yarn ng build --configuration=production
        echo "=== Largest JS/CSS files (public/browser) ==="
        find public -type f \( -name "*.js" -o -name "*.css" \) \
          -exec du -h {} \; | sort -rh | head -25
        echo "=== Total bundle size ==="
        du -sh public/browser || du -sh public
      continue-on-error: true
README.md — changed (Firebase → Pages, new badge)
markdown
# Colorado Quiz

A small Angular 22 PWA of quizzes and news, deployed to GitHub Pages at
https://angulareighteen.github.io.

[![Deploy to GitHub Pages](https://github.com/angulareighteen/angulareighteen.github.io/actions/workflows/deploy.yml/badge.svg)](https://github.com/angulareighteen/angulareighteen.github.io/actions/workflows/deploy.yml)

## Tech stack

- **Angular 22** — standalone components, **signals**, **zoneless** change detection
  (no `zone.js`), the built-in control flow (`@if` / `@for`), and lazy-loaded routes.
- **Angular Material 22** with a Material 3 theme (`mat.theme`).
- **Vitest** + **jsdom** for unit tests (replaces Karma/Jasmine).
- **Playwright** for end-to-end tests (replaces Protractor).
- **@angular/service-worker** for PWA/offline support.

## Prerequisites

- Node.js `^22.22.3 || ^24.15.0 || >=26` (matches the Angular 22 CLI engine range)
- Yarn (classic)

## Commands

```bash
yarn install            # install dependencies
yarn start              # dev server at http://localhost:4200
yarn build              # production build -> public/browser (published to GitHub Pages)
yarn test               # unit tests (Vitest, watch mode)
yarn test:ci            # unit tests once, with coverage
yarn e2e                # end-to-end tests (Playwright)
```

The first time you run e2e on a machine, install the browser once:

```bash
yarn playwright install chromium
```

## Deployment

Deployment is automatic via GitHub Actions. On every push to `master`, the
`.github/workflows/deploy.yml` workflow builds the app (`ng build
--configuration=production`), publishes `public/browser` to GitHub Pages, and
the site goes live at https://angulareighteen.github.io.

The workflow also copies `index.html` to `404.html` so client-side routes (deep
links such as `/quiz/us-civics`) resolve correctly — GitHub Pages has no
server-side rewrites — and writes a `.nojekyll` file so the output is served
verbatim.

One-time setup: in the repository, go to **Settings → Pages → Build and
deployment** and set **Source** to **GitHub Actions**.

You can also trigger a deploy manually from the **Actions** tab
(**Deploy to GitHub Pages → Run workflow**).
A couple of notes
I left angular.json's outputPath: "public" alone — the deploy workflow auto-detects public/browser, so it keeps working as-is. If you'd rather move to the Angular default (dist/), I can adjust the workflow and firebase-era naming to match; just say so.
Since you're fully off Firebase, karma.conf.js and tslint.json are also dead weight (you're on Vitest + Prettier now). Not touching them here, but they're safe to delete whenever.
On the token: I only used it for read-only version lookups against the GitHub API and didn't store it anywhere. It's now in this chat's history, so rotating it at some point is still the clean move even though it's read-only.
Want me to also wire the deploy so it only runs after test.yml passes, or is push-to-master deploys fine as-is?
